title: Intimate Partner Violence
description: Percentage of women aged 15 to 49 years who have ever had a husband or intimate partner who have ever experienced emotional, physical, or sexual violence committed by their current or most recent husband/intimate partner, according to background characteristics: Philippines, 2022 and 2025

region.psa_note: Region IX includes the province of Sulu in 2025.

footnotes: ² Region IX includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
“NA” denotes that an estimate is not reported.
"0.0" indicates that an estimate is less than 0.05 percent.
notes: 1. The term husband includes a partner with whom a woman is living as if married. Husband/intimate partner refers to the current husband for currently married women, the most recent husband for divorced, separated or widowed women, the current intimate partner for never married women who currently have an intimate partner, and the most recent intimate partner for never married women who do not currently have an intimate partner but had one in the past.
2. Details may not add up to total due to rounding.
tableData.source: Philippine Statistics Authority, 2022 and 2025 National Demographic and Health Survey

insights.title: Insights on Intimate Partner Violence
insights.subtitle: AI-generated insights based on National Demographic and Health Survey data
insights.overview: At national level, indicators of intimate partner violence declined from 2022 to 2025, but that improvement sits on top of very wide regional and marital-status divides. The share of women who ever experienced physical or sexual or emotional violence fell from 17.5 to 15.4, and physical or sexual violence fell from 7.3 to 5.8. Yet in 2025 the headline regional range is still large, from 3.2 in BARMM to 24.0 in Caraga. At the same time, marital-status contrast is extreme: divorced/separated/widowed women are at 41.4 on the headline indicator, compared with 13.9 among married/living together women and 7.4 among never-married women who currently have an intimate partner. The strongest reading is therefore dual: broad national decline, but persistent structural concentration by place and relationship history.

sections.heading: Regional spread remains large even after national decline
sections.body: In 2025, physical or sexual or emotional violence ranges from 3.2 in BARMM to 24.0 in Caraga. Bicol (23.9), Zamboanga Peninsula (21.1), Central Luzon (20.5), and Eastern Visayas (20.3) also sit high, while Central Visayas (9.2), NIR (11.7), and Ilocos Region (12.8) are much lower. This spread is far wider than the national year-to-year change and shows that national averages continue to compress very different regional conditions.

sections.heading: Regional change from 2022 to 2025 is mixed, not synchronized
sections.body: Some regions show large declines in the headline indicator, especially Ilocos Region (25.0 to 12.8), Central Visayas (18.6 to 9.2), and CAR (19.8 to 14.5). Others moved in the opposite direction, most notably Caraga (20.2 to 24.0) and Central Luzon (17.0 to 20.5), with smaller increases in Zamboanga Peninsula (20.7 to 21.1). This pattern indicates that the national decline is an aggregate of divergent regional trajectories rather than parallel improvement everywhere.

sections.heading: Regional profiles differ by component, not only by headline level
sections.body: High-headline regions are not identical in composition. Caraga combines very high emotional violence (21.8) with elevated physical (6.8) and sexual (3.7) violence. Bicol is high across all three major components, including physical violence at 9.1. Central Luzon's 20.5 headline is driven heavily by emotional violence (18.9) relative to lower sexual violence (2.1). BARMM is low across components (2.9 emotional, 0.7 physical, 0.4 sexual), producing a low headline level. Composition differences matter because they imply different mixes beneath similar totals.

sections.heading: Marital status is one of the strongest structural divides in the entire table
sections.body: In 2025, divorced/separated/widowed women are at 41.4 for physical or sexual or emotional violence, 18.3 for physical or sexual violence, 17.3 for physical violence, and 6.7 for sexual violence. Married/living together women are much lower (13.9 headline, 5.4 physical or sexual, 5.0 physical, 1.5 sexual). Among never-married women, current-partner and past-partner groups are also very different: 7.4 headline among those with a current intimate partner versus 33.4 among those who had a partner in the past. These are very large separations and should be read as central, not peripheral, to the IPV pattern.

sections.heading: Marital-status gaps remain large despite declines from 2022
sections.body: The highest-risk marital-status groups improved in 2025 but remained far above other groups. Divorced/separated/widowed women declined from 44.6 to 41.4 on the headline indicator, and never-married women with a past partner rose sharply from 18.6 to 33.4. Married/living together women declined from 17.2 to 13.9, and never-married women with a current partner remained lower at 7.4 (from 6.7). So the national decline did not flatten relationship-history differences; it coexists with persistent and in some cases widening gaps between marital-status subgroups.

sections.heading: Residence and wealth show gradients, but smaller than regional and marital-status gaps
sections.body: Rural women remain higher than urban women on the headline indicator (15.9 vs 15.0) and physical or sexual violence (6.4 vs 5.3), and both urban and rural declined versus 2022. Wealth follows a clearer gradient in 2025: 19.1 in the lowest quintile versus 10.6 in the highest on the headline measure, with similar separation for physical violence (7.4 vs 2.4). These gradients are meaningful, but they are still smaller than the largest regional and marital-status contrasts.

sections.heading: Age differences are moderate compared with other dimensions
sections.body: Across ages 15-49, 2025 headline levels cluster in a relatively narrow band (14.3 to 16.1). Physical violence ranges from 3.3 in ages 15-19 to 6.3 in ages 25-29, then moderates. Emotional violence is slightly higher in ages 25-29 and 40-49 than in other groups. The age profile matters, but it is less polarizing than region or marital status.

sections.heading: Religion, ethnicity, education, and employment add texture but include caution flags
sections.body: Subgroup variation is visible across these dimensions. In 2025, headline levels are high among Aglipay (23.1) and Other religion (24.3), and among Bikolano (22.2) and Waray (21.4) ethnic groups. Educational attainment also varies, with higher headline levels in Grades 1-6 (20.2) than in college (11.4). Employment groups are close to each other (15.2 to 15.5). Some cells include NA, asterisks, or very small totals, so extreme subgroup values should be interpreted cautiously.

takeaways: National IPV indicators declined from 2022 to 2025, but regional and marital-status inequalities remain substantial.
Regional trajectories diverge: some regions fell sharply while others increased, so national progress is not geographically uniform.
Marital-status structure is a core driver of disparity, with divorced/separated/widowed women and never-married women with past partners showing much higher prevalence.
Rural and lower-wealth groups remain more exposed on average, but their gaps are generally smaller than the largest regional and marital-status differences.
